sorting.title: Sorting Algorithms
sorting.description: Visualization demonstrations of various classic sorting algorithms

bubbleSort.name: Bubble Sort
bubbleSort.description: Repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order, gradually moving larger elements to the end of the array
bubbleSort.principle: Bubble Sort is one of the simplest sorting algorithms. It repeatedly traverses the array to be sorted, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. After each pass, the largest element will be moved to its correct position, gradually floating up like bubbles in water.
steps: Start from the first element of the array and compare adjacent elements
If the previous element is greater than the next, swap them
Move to the next pair of adjacent elements and repeat the comparison and swap process
After completing one pass, the largest element has been moved to the end of the array
Repeat the above process for all elements except the last one
Continue this process until the entire array is sorted
bubbleSort.timeComplexity: Average time complexity is O(n²), worst case (reverse order) is O(n²), best case (already sorted) is O(n)
bubbleSort.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(1), an in-place sorting algorithm
bubbleSort.scenarios: Suitable for small data sets or nearly sorted data. Due to its simple implementation, it's often used for teaching demonstrations. Easy to understand and debug.
bubbleSort.example: For array [5, 2, 8, 1, 9]: First pass compare 5 and 2 (swap) → [2, 5, 8, 1, 9], compare 5 and 8 (no swap), compare 8 and 1 (swap) → [2, 5, 1, 8, 9], compare 8 and 9 (no swap)

selectionSort.name: Selection Sort
selectionSort.description: Select the minimum element from the unsorted part and place it at the end of the sorted part
selectionSort.principle: Selection Sort works by finding the minimum (or maximum) element in the unsorted sequence and placing it at the end of the sorted sequence. It first assumes the first element is the minimum, then compares it with each subsequent element, records the position of the minimum, and after one pass, swaps the found minimum with the current position.
steps: Find the index of the minimum element in the unsorted array
Swap this minimum element with the first element of the unsorted part
Expand the sorted part by one element
Repeat the above process for the remaining unsorted part
Continue until all elements are sorted
selectionSort.timeComplexity: Time complexity is always O(n²), regardless of the initial state of the array
selectionSort.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(1), an in-place sorting algorithm
selectionSort.scenarios: Suitable for simple scenarios or small-scale data sorting. When swap costs are high, it's better than bubble sort due to fewer swaps.
selectionSort.example: Array [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]: First pass finds minimum value 11, swaps to position 0 → [11, 25, 12, 22, 64]

insertionSort.name: Insertion Sort
insertionSort.description: Insert elements into the correct position in the sorted part, similar to organizing playing cards
insertionSort.principle: Insertion Sort works similarly to organizing playing cards. The array is divided into a sorted part and an unsorted part. Each time an element is taken from the unsorted part, it's inserted into its correct position in the sorted part. It assumes the first element is sorted, then processes remaining elements one by one.
steps: Treat the first element of the array as the sorted part
Take the first element from the unsorted part
Scan the sorted part from right to left
Move elements in the sorted part that are greater than the taken element backward
Insert the taken element into its correct position
Repeat until all elements have been processed
insertionSort.timeComplexity: Best case O(n) (already sorted), average and worst case O(n²)
insertionSort.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(1), an in-place sorting algorithm
insertionSort.scenarios: Very efficient when data is nearly sorted or when data size is small. Suitable for online sorting (continuously receiving new data). Often used as a subprocess in hybrid sorting algorithms.
insertionSort.example: Array [12, 11, 13, 5, 6]: When processing 11, compare with 12 and swap → [11, 12, 13, 5, 6], when processing 13, position unchanged

quickSort.name: Quick Sort
quickSort.description: Select a pivot element, partition the array into two parts, and recursively sort
quickSort.principle: Quick Sort uses a divide-and-conquer strategy. Choose a pivot element (usually first, last, or middle element), partition the array into two parts: elements less than the pivot on the left, elements greater than or equal to the pivot on the right. Then recursively apply the same operation to both parts.
steps: Select a pivot element
Rearrange the array so elements less than pivot are on the left, elements greater than or equal to pivot are on the right
Recursively apply Quick Sort to the left part
Recursively apply Quick Sort to the right part
Stop recursion when subarray size is 0 or 1
quickSort.timeComplexity: Average O(n log n), worst case O(n²) (when data is nearly sorted and first element is chosen as pivot)
quickSort.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(log n) (recursion stack depth)
quickSort.scenarios: Suitable for large-scale data sorting, the most commonly used sorting algorithm in practice. Worst-case scenarios can be avoided by choosing pivots wisely (random or median-of-three).
quickSort.example: Array [10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5], choosing 5 as pivot, rearranged to → [1, 5, 8, 9, 10, 7], then recursively sort [1] and [8, 9, 10, 7]

mergeSort.name: Merge Sort
mergeSort.description: Divide-and-conquer strategy, recursively split the array and merge into a sorted array
mergeSort.principle: Merge Sort uses a divide-and-conquer approach. First recursively divide the array into smallest subarrays (each containing only one element), then gradually merge these subarrays in sorted order, finally obtaining a complete sorted array.
steps: Split the array from the middle into two subarrays
Recursively perform merge sort on both subarrays
Merge two sorted subarrays into one sorted array
Repeat the merge process until only one array remains
mergeSort.timeComplexity: Time complexity is always O(n log n), independent of the initial data state
mergeSort.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(n), requiring additional arrays for merging results
mergeSort.scenarios: Suitable for scenarios requiring stable sorting and worst-case guarantees. Very useful in external sorting (like large file sorting). Good for linked list sorting, can achieve O(n log n) time and O(1) space complexity.
mergeSort.example: Array [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]: Split into [38, 27, 43] and [3, 9, 82, 10], continue splitting, finally merge and sort during merge

heapSort.name: Heap Sort
heapSort.description: Use heap data structure, first build a max heap, then extract maximum elements gradually
heapSort.principle: Heap Sort uses the heap data structure, a complete binary tree. First, build the array into a max heap (each node's value is greater than or equal to its children), then repeatedly swap the max heap root (maximum) with the heap tail element, shrink the heap size (excluding the sorted part), and finally adjust the heap to maintain max heap property.
steps: Build the array into a max heap (heapification)
Swap the heap root (maximum) with the heap tail element
Reduce heap size by one (sorted part increases by one)
Adjust the root downward to restore max heap property
Repeat steps 2-4 until heap size is 1
heapSort.timeComplexity: Time complexity is always O(n log n), independent of the initial data state
heapSort.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(1), an in-place sorting algorithm
heapSort.scenarios: Suitable for scenarios requiring O(n log n) time complexity with O(1) space complexity. Commonly used to implement priority queues. Good for memory-constrained environments.
heapSort.example: Array [4, 10, 3, 5, 1] after building max heap: [10, 5, 3, 4, 1], swap 10 and 1, adjust heap, finally get sorted array [1, 3, 4, 5, 10]

search.title: Search Algorithms
search.description: Visualization demonstrations of common search algorithms

binarySearch.name: Binary Search
binarySearch.description: In a sorted array, quickly locate the target element by halving the search range
binarySearch.principle: Binary Search is suitable for sorted arrays. Each search halves the search range: compare the middle element with the target value. If equal, found the target. If target is smaller, continue searching in the left half. If target is larger, continue searching in the right half.
steps: Determine the left and right boundaries of the search range
Calculate middle position: mid = (left + right) / 2
Compare middle element with target value
If equal, return middle position (found target)
If target is smaller, narrow right boundary to mid-1
If target is larger, narrow left boundary to mid+1
Repeat steps 2-6 until target is found or range is empty
binarySearch.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(1) (iterative) or O(log n) (recursive)
binarySearch.scenarios: Suitable for static searching (ordered data with infrequent insertions/deletions). Such as dictionary lookup, database indexing, spell checking, etc.
binarySearch.example: Search for target value 7 in sorted array [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13]: mid=5 (value 9) > 7, narrow right boundary; mid=2 (value 5) < 7, expand left boundary; mid=3 (value 7), found target

linearSearch.name: Linear Search
linearSearch.description: Traverse the array sequentially, comparing each element to find the target
linearSearch.principle: Linear Search is the simplest search algorithm. Starting from the first element of the array, compare each element with the target value. If found, return the element position. If the entire array is traversed without finding, return failure.
steps: Start from the first element of the array
Compare the current element with the target value
If equal, return current position (found target)
Move to the next element
Repeat steps 2-4 until target is found or end of array reached
linearSearch.timeComplexity: Best case O(1), average and worst case O(n)
linearSearch.scenarios: Suitable for unordered or small-scale data. When data is irregular and insertions/deletions are frequent. Can also be used when search frequency is very low.
linearSearch.example: Search for target value 22 in array [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]: Start comparing from index 0, 64 ≠ 22, 25 ≠ 22, 12 ≠ 22, 22 = 22, found target at index 3

tree.title: Tree Structures
tree.description: Binary trees and related operation visualizations

binaryTree.name: Binary Tree Traversal
binaryTree.description: Pre-order, in-order, and post-order traversal of binary trees
binaryTree.principle: Binary tree traversal is the foundation of tree operations, with three main methods: Pre-order (Root-Left-Right), In-order (Left-Root-Right), Post-order (Left-Right-Root). The traversal order refers to when the root node is visited.
binaryTree.steps: Traversal process is recursive, each node is visited once

preorder.name: Pre-order Traversal
preorder.description: Visit root first, then recursively traverse left subtree, then recursively traverse right subtree
steps: Visit current node (root)
Recursively traverse left subtree
Recursively traverse right subtree
preorder.principle: Pre-order traversal visits in "Root-Left-Right" order. This traversal is commonly used for creating or copying binary trees and obtaining prefix expressions of trees.
preorder.scenarios: Used for tree copying and serialization. Generate prefix expressions (like prefix notation of mathematical expressions). Create backups of directory structures.

inorder.name: In-order Traversal
inorder.description: First recursively traverse left subtree, then visit root, then recursively traverse right subtree
steps: Recursively traverse left subtree
Visit current node (root)
Recursively traverse right subtree
inorder.principle: In-order traversal visits in "Left-Root-Right" order. For binary search trees, in-order traversal visits all nodes in ascending order.
inorder.scenarios: In-order traversal of binary search tree yields sorted sequence. Obtain infix expressions from expression trees. Used in parsers for syntax analysis.

postorder.name: Post-order Traversal
postorder.description: First recursively traverse left subtree, then recursively traverse right subtree, then visit root
steps: Recursively traverse left subtree
Recursively traverse right subtree
Visit current node (root)
postorder.principle: Post-order traversal visits in "Left-Right-Root" order. This ensures that all child nodes have been processed before visiting the root node.
postorder.scenarios: Used for releasing binary trees (release children first, then parent). Calculate sizes of directories and subdirectories. Generate postfix expressions of trees.

graph.title: Graph Algorithms
graph.description: Graph traversal and shortest path algorithms

bfs.name: Breadth-First Search
bfs.description: Starting from the source, expand the search layer by layer, suitable for finding shortest paths
bfs.principle: Breadth-First Search uses a queue to traverse the graph. Starting from the source node, first visit all adjacent nodes, then visit adjacent nodes of those nodes, expanding layer by layer. This ensures that the path from source to any node is the shortest (in unweighted graphs).
steps: Add source node to queue
Remove a node from queue
Visit all unvisited neighbors of this node
Add unvisited neighbors to queue
Repeat steps 2-4 until queue is empty
bfs.timeComplexity: Time complexity is O(V + E), V is number of vertices, E is number of edges
bfs.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(V), needs to store queue and visited markers
bfs.scenarios: Finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs. "N-degree friends" queries in social networks. Web crawlers in search engines. Maze solving (find shortest path).
bfs.example: Starting BFS from node A: A enters queue → dequeue and visit A, A's neighbors B, C enter queue → dequeue B, visit B, B's neighbor D enters queue → dequeue C, visit C... (layer by layer)

dfs.name: Depth-First Search
dfs.description: Go as deep as possible along one path, then backtrack to explore other paths
dfs.principle: Depth-First Search uses a stack (recursive call stack) to traverse the graph. Starting from the source node, go as deep as possible along one path until unable to continue, then backtrack to the nearest branch point and explore other paths.
steps: Start from source, mark as visited and push to stack
Look for unvisited neighbors of the top node in stack
If unvisited neighbor exists, visit it and push to stack
If no unvisited neighbor exists, pop from stack
Repeat steps 2-4 until stack is empty
dfs.timeComplexity: Time complexity is O(V + E), V is number of vertices, E is number of edges
dfs.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(V), needs to store stack and visited markers
dfs.scenarios: Detecting connected components in graphs. Topological sorting. Solving maze problems (not guaranteed shortest path). Depth-first traversal. Cycle detection.
dfs.example: Starting DFS from node A: Visit A, A's neighbor B, visit B, B's neighbor C, visit C... go deep to leaf, then backtrack, explore other branches

dijkstra.name: Dijkstra's Shortest Path
dijkstra.description: Calculate the shortest path from source to all other nodes
dijkstra.principle: Dijkstra's algorithm calculates single-source shortest paths (from one source to all other nodes). It uses a greedy strategy: each time, select the unvisited node with the minimum current distance, then update distances of its neighbors.
steps: Initialize: set source distance to 0, other nodes' distances to infinity
Select the unvisited node with minimum distance
Visit this node
For each neighbor of this node, calculate distance through this node
If calculated distance is less than known distance, update distance
Repeat steps 2-5 until all nodes are visited
dijkstra.timeComplexity: With heap optimization: O((V + E) log V)
dijkstra.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(V), needs to store distances and visited state
dijkstra.scenarios: Shortest path calculation in GPS navigation systems. Routing in computer networks. Flight schedule analysis. Logistics distribution route optimization.
dijkstra.example: Starting from point A: A distance = 0, update B = 2, C = 3 → select B → update D = 4, E = 6 → select C → update D = 3 (smaller) → continue until all points processed

datastructure.title: Data Structures
datastructure.description: Operation demonstrations of basic data structures

stack.name: Stack
stack.principle: A stack is a special linear data structure that only allows insertion and deletion at one end (the top). Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle: the last element entering the stack is the first to be popped out.

operations.push: Push: Add element to top of stack
operations.pop: Pop: Remove and return top element
operations.peek: Peek: View top element without removing
operations.isEmpty: Is Empty: Check if stack is empty

stack.timeComplexity: All operations have O(1) time complexity
stack.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(n), where n is number of elements in stack
stack.scenarios: Function call stack (recursive calls). Undo operations (editors). Bracket matching. Expression evaluation (calculators). Backtracking algorithms.

queue.name: Queue
queue.principle: A queue is a special linear data structure following the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle. Elements are added at the tail and removed from the head. Like people standing in line to buy tickets: the first person in line is served first.

operations.enqueue: Enqueue: Add element to tail of queue
operations.dequeue: Dequeue: Remove and return head element
operations.front: Front: View head element without removing
operations.isEmpty: Is Empty: Check if queue is empty

queue.timeComplexity: All operations have O(1) time complexity
queue.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(n), where n is number of elements in queue
queue.scenarios: Task scheduling (operating systems). Message queues. BFS graph traversal. Print task queue. Network request queue.

linkedList.name: Linked List
linkedList.description: A sequence of nodes connected by pointers
linkedList.principle: A linked list is a linear data structure where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node (singly linked list). Nodes don't need to be stored contiguously; they're linked together through pointers.

operations.insert: Insert: Insert new node at specified position
operations.delete: Delete: Delete node at specified position

linkedList.timeComplexity: O(1) for head operations, O(n) for search and position-based operations
linkedList.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(n), needs to store data and pointers
linkedList.scenarios: Implementing other data structures (stacks, queues). Dynamic memory allocation. Music playlists. Browser history (forward/backward).

message.initial: Add operations and click "Start" to execute

search.title: Search Algorithms
search.description: Visualization demonstrations of common search algorithms
search.setTarget: Set target value and click "Start Search"

binarySearch.name: Binary Search
binarySearch.description: In a sorted array, quickly locate the target element by halving the search range
binarySearch.principle: Binary Search is suitable for sorted arrays. Each search halves the search range: compare the middle element with the target value. If equal, found the target. If target is smaller, continue searching in the left half. If target is larger, continue searching in the right half.
steps: Determine the left and right boundaries of the search range
Calculate middle position: mid = (left + right) / 2
Compare middle element with target value
If equal, return middle position (found target)
If target is smaller, narrow right boundary to mid-1
If target is larger, narrow left boundary to mid+1
Repeat steps 2-6 until target is found or range is empty
binarySearch.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(1) (iterative) or O(log n) (recursive)
binarySearch.scenarios: Suitable for static searching (ordered data with infrequent insertions/deletions). Such as dictionary lookup, database indexing, spell checking, etc.
binarySearch.example: Search for target value 7 in sorted array [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13]: mid=5 (value 9) > 7, narrow right boundary; mid=2 (value 5) < 7, expand left boundary; mid=3 (value 7), found target
binarySearch.info: Binary search requires the array to be sorted. Each time the search range is halved to quickly locate the target element.

linearSearch.name: Linear Search
linearSearch.description: Traverse the array sequentially, comparing each element to find the target
linearSearch.principle: Linear Search is the simplest search algorithm. Starting from the first element of the array, compare each element with the target value. If found, return the element position. If the entire array is traversed without finding, return failure.
steps: Start from the first element of the array
Compare the current element with the target value
If equal, return current position (found target)
Move to the next element
Repeat steps 2-4 until target is found or end of array reached
linearSearch.timeComplexity: Best case O(1), average and worst case O(n)
linearSearch.scenarios: Suitable for unordered or small-scale data. When data is irregular and insertions/deletions are frequent. Can also be used when search frequency is very low.
linearSearch.example: Search for target value 22 in array [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]: Start comparing from index 0, 64 ≠ 22, 25 ≠ 22, 12 ≠ 22, 22 = 22, found target at index 3

tree.title: Tree Structures
tree.description: Binary trees and related operation visualizations
tree.selectType: Select traversal type and click "Start Traversal"

binaryTree.name: Binary Tree Traversal
binaryTree.description: Pre-order, in-order, and post-order traversal of binary trees
binaryTree.principle: Binary tree traversal is the foundation of tree operations, with three main methods: Pre-order (Root-Left-Right), In-order (Left-Root-Right), Post-order (Left-Right-Root). The traversal order refers to when the root node is visited.
binaryTree.steps: Traversal process is recursive, each node is visited once

preorder.name: Preorder
preorder.description: Order: Root → Left Subtree → Right Subtree
steps: Visit current node (root)
Recursively traverse left subtree
Recursively traverse right subtree
preorder.principle: Pre-order traversal visits in "Root-Left-Right" order. This traversal is commonly used for creating or copying binary trees and obtaining prefix expressions of trees.
preorder.scenarios: Used for tree copying and serialization. Generate prefix expressions (like prefix notation of mathematical expressions). Create backups of directory structures.

inorder.name: Inorder
inorder.description: Order: Left Subtree → Root → Right Subtree
steps: Recursively traverse left subtree
Visit current node (root)
Recursively traverse right subtree
inorder.principle: In-order traversal visits in "Left-Root-Right" order. For binary search trees, in-order traversal visits all nodes in ascending order.
inorder.scenarios: In-order traversal of binary search tree yields sorted sequence. Obtain infix expressions from expression trees. Used in parsers for syntax analysis.

postorder.name: Postorder
postorder.description: Order: Left Subtree → Right Subtree → Root
steps: Recursively traverse left subtree
Recursively traverse right subtree
Visit current node (root)
postorder.principle: Post-order traversal visits in "Left-Right-Root" order. This ensures that all child nodes have been processed before visiting the root node.
postorder.scenarios: Used for releasing binary trees (release children first, then parent). Calculate sizes of directories and subdirectories. Generate postfix expressions of trees.

graph.title: Graph Algorithms
graph.description: Graph traversal and shortest path algorithms

bfs.name: Breadth-First Search
bfs.description: Starting from the source, expand the search layer by layer, suitable for finding shortest paths
bfs.principle: Breadth-First Search uses a queue to traverse the graph. Starting from the source node, first visit all adjacent nodes, then visit adjacent nodes of those nodes, expanding layer by layer. This ensures that the path from source to any node is the shortest (in unweighted graphs).
steps: Add source node to queue
Remove a node from queue
Visit all unvisited neighbors of this node
Add unvisited neighbors to queue
Repeat steps 2-4 until queue is empty
bfs.timeComplexity: Time complexity is O(V + E), V is number of vertices, E is number of edges
bfs.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(V), needs to store queue and visited markers
bfs.scenarios: Finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs. "N-degree friends" queries in social networks. Web crawlers in search engines. Maze solving (find shortest path).
bfs.example: Starting BFS from node A: A enters queue → dequeue and visit A, A's neighbors B, C enter queue → dequeue B, visit B, B's neighbor D enters queue → dequeue C, visit C... (layer by layer)

dfs.name: Depth-First Search
dfs.description: Go as deep as possible along one path, then backtrack to explore other paths
dfs.principle: Depth-First Search uses a stack (recursive call stack) to traverse the graph. Starting from the source node, go as deep as possible along one path until unable to continue, then backtrack to the nearest branch point and explore other paths.
steps: Start from source, mark as visited and push to stack
Look for unvisited neighbors of the top node in stack
If unvisited neighbor exists, visit it and push to stack
If no unvisited neighbor exists, pop from stack
Repeat steps 2-4 until stack is empty
dfs.timeComplexity: Time complexity is O(V + E), V is number of vertices, E is number of edges
dfs.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(V), needs to store stack and visited markers
dfs.scenarios: Detecting connected components in graphs. Topological sorting. Solving maze problems (not guaranteed shortest path). Depth-first traversal. Cycle detection.
dfs.example: Starting DFS from node A: Visit A, A's neighbor B, visit B, B's neighbor C, visit C... go deep to leaf, then backtrack, explore other branches

dijkstra.name: Dijkstra's Shortest Path
dijkstra.description: Calculate the shortest path from source to all other nodes
dijkstra.principle: Dijkstra's algorithm calculates single-source shortest paths (from one source to all other nodes). It uses a greedy strategy: each time, select the unvisited node with the minimum current distance, then update distances of its neighbors.
steps: Initialize: set source distance to 0, other nodes' distances to infinity
Select the unvisited node with minimum distance
Visit this node
For each neighbor of this node, calculate distance through this node
If calculated distance is less than known distance, update distance
Repeat steps 2-5 until all nodes are visited
dijkstra.timeComplexity: With heap optimization: O((V + E) log V)
dijkstra.spaceComplexity: Space complexity is O(V), needs to store distances and visited state
dijkstra.scenarios: Shortest path calculation in GPS navigation systems. Routing in computer networks. Flight schedule analysis. Logistics distribution route optimization.
dijkstra.example: Starting from point A: A distance = 0, update B = 2, C = 3 → select B → update D = 4, E = 6 → select C → update D = 3 (smaller) → continue until all points processed
